question: why is it called 'dry run' ?

dry-run is usually a way to validate a request without performing the intended operation of the API. Here we are validating the data without persisting it to the database.
i found this article, a nice short explaination: https://dx.apiture.com/docs/api/concepts/dry-runs/

inline_comments.message:
any reason for validating it twice ???
is it because after invalidating the cache , you want to make a DB hit so that the cache is populated again ??
NB: added because of sonar complaining??

It's the same as the existing code on line 59.

It's because the Yang cache (yangTextSchemaSourceSetCache) is not a hazelcast cache, it is a caffeine cache, local to each instance only. So if a schema is updated/deleted on one CPS instance, the other instance cache would be out of sync. So try to validate first, if it fails invalidate the cache and retry. If it still fails after cache refresh, then it is a real failure.
I am curious if this and other new methods are really needed. The existing method 'parseData' already validates during parsing, no?

yes we could use it but only for JSON parsing. For parsing XML there are 2 steps involved. 
1. XML is parsed
2. The parsed data is packaged into a container node, this step prevents the parsing and validation of leaf nodes.
Earlier this step was being performed as part of the XML parsing but I have extracted it to a separate method in this patch. YangParserHelper.java line 194

NB: just thinking , would it be possible to have primitive boolean instead of Boolean ??
boolean is just true/false. ( false by default )
Boolean can as well be null.
Not tried this , but if u want to check it.

it is not possible to use the primitive type because after defining the parameter in components.yml the generated CpsDataApi.java file uses the wrapper class by default for boolean values.
Hence to handle null values and avoid the null pointer exception I used 
if (Boolean.TRUE.equals(dryRunEnabled)) on line 76.

To ensure the generated Java code uses primitive types like boolean instead of wrapper types like Boolean, you need to set the usePrimitiveType property in the OpenAPI Maven plugin configuration in your pom.xml.

 <!-- This ensures primitive types like boolean are used -->
 <usePrimitiveType>true</usePrimitiveType>
 
 
Use boolean primitive instead of Boolean: Since dryRunEnabled only accepts true/false values and doesn't need null-checks, a boolean primitive is preferable.

I tried this but <usePrimitiveType>true</usePrimitiveType> did not work instead I used <typeMappings>typeMapping>boolean=boolean</typeMapping></typeMappings>.
But a problem with this approach will be that it impacts the entire rest layer and will change all Boolean types to boolean, this includes the parameter "includeDescendants" in other controller methods like "getNodeByDataspaceAndAnchor" and "getNodesByDataspaceAndAnchorAndCpsPath".
So, I suggest we use Boolean for now as null condition is handled in this change. We can have a separate patch converting Boolean to boolean which could also handle other methods and also cover its impact on those methods (if any) in that patch itself.
yangParser is an object rather than a mock in CpsDataServiceImplSpec, so using 1 * yangParser.parseData(anchor, xpath, data, contentType) throws error with no invocations.
yes.. we can have a separate patch for this. will remove unnecessary null checks.

This question was not brought up by anyone in the discussions. But from my study what I found is the most common scenario in dry-run application is to return either a 200 or 204 response code with no content. And I kept the same in my proposal document.
Any inputs are appreciated.

yes we can use the existing method, the same question was asked by Daniel earlier(https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/cps/+/138676/comment/c49d4cc2_777f68bf/). 
But there is a slight difference in the parser methods of JSON and XML data.XML parsing performs an extra step as follows
1. XML is parsed
2. The parsed data is packaged into a container node, this step prevents the parsing and validation of leaf nodes.
So, if we use the existing method, the test, https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/cps/+/138676/19/cps-service/src/test/groovy/org/onap/cps/api/impl/CpsDataServiceImplSpec.groovy#548 would lead to a failure for scenario 4: 'XML data with specific xpath' which is validating a leaf node under a specific xpath.

In the current implementation, the above two steps are performed /cps-service/src/main/java/org/onap/cps/utils/YangParserHelper.java#130
changed it to validateData as recommended in the following comment https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/cps/+/138676/comment/3ec1b344_06c9a33f/

this is the test that helped identify that XML parsing would fail when validating individual leaves. as mentioned in the following comment https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/cps/+/138676/comment/3ec1b344_06c9a33f/
Specifically, the 4th scenario. So what should we do? keep the entire test or remove the 3rd one and keep 1st 2nd and 4th.
I woudl like to see a differnce so maybe a 204 is a good idea. Lets make suer we dsicuss this soon with Priyank etc. But it does not block this commit.

actually, I stil dont understand this really: if XML validation failed in that second part why are you EXCLUDING it from the validation logic?!
you shoudl have done similar in teh production code :-)

the second part does not deal with validation of XML, rather one can say it handles data extraction. As I said earlier XML parsing is done in 2 steps:
1. We parse the XML data and validate it against a Yang model(this is the only step needed for validation). Once the XML is successfully parsed the XML structure after parsing contains a top-level container node corresponding to "root". This root is not part of the core XML data that was provided as part of request (just in memory representation by ODL). So, we move to second step, that is to extract the first child under root using getFirstChildXmlRoot().
2. This step involves extracting the first child under root node. The extraction is handled by getFirstChildXmlRoot() and we can see it ignores leaf nodes (as seen on line 260 !(child instanceof LeafNode)) because usually any meaningful data is under container nodes or list nodes. So if we provide just a leaf node it fails in this step.

So, if a validation request contains only leaves, then the yang parser successfully parses it because they might be a valid element in the associated Yang model. But fails in second step that is extraction of core data under the root node.
yes I was going for it but changed it to !VALIDATE_ONLY, right before pushing the code. Fixed it now 😄

ok thanks for explaining so clearly

currently there is no configuration in place (within the pom.xml files) for the kind of boolean value that will be set for generated java code. So, by default the CpsDataApi.java uses Boolean wrapper class instead of boolean primitive.
So here final Boolean dryRunEnabled can be true, false or null. Therefore, to handle the null condition I used Boolean.TRUE.equals(dryRunEnabled) because in case where dryRunEnabled is null, the equals expression will result in a "false" and hence it will consider the dry-run to be off and execute the else block.
I suggested a fix in the following comment https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/cps/+/138676/comment/76be3815_3b9b1a94/ we decided to not implement it in the current patch because this change will impact the parameter "includeDescendants" in other controller methods like "getNodeByDataspaceAndAnchor" and "getNodesByDataspaceAndAnchorAndCpsPath".
